Everything I Wish I KnewAbout My Cycle & Hormones

Unlearn and Finally Heal

∞Transformation
Before We Begin
You Were Designed With Purpose

You were created by God, in His image, with extraordinary intentionality. Every part of your body was designed with meaning and wisdom far greater than the medical system has ever fully understood.

On the days when it feels hard, when old habits feel impossible to break, when progress feels slow, I want you to pray. Talk to God about your body, your healing, your frustration. He cares about every part of you, including this. And believe in yourself too. Your body already knows how to heal. It has always known. What we are doing in this guide is not creating something new. We are removing what is in the way, and giving your body the conditions it was always designed to thrive in.

"The Spirit of God, who raised Jesus from the dead, lives in you."
Romans 8:11

Your body is a temple, and it is working with you. When you begin to understand your cycle through that lens, everything will shift.

· The pain will become a message ·
· The fatigue will become an invitation to rest ·
· The emotions will become signals ·
Course Modules
Module 01 · Introduction

Welcome, Beautiful Woman 🌸

This guide is everything I wish someone had handed me years ago before the pain, the confusion, and the years of just dealing with it.

A Letter to You
"You are not broken. You were just never given the truth about your own body, and convinced that you had no power over it."

If you have spent years suffering through painful periods, mood swings, exhaustion before your cycle, bloating, or just feeling completely unlike yourself, this guide is for you. You were handed birth control or ibuprofen and told this is just part of being a woman. You were never taught how your hormones actually work. So let's learn together.

✿ What This Guide Covers
  • The four phases of your cycle and what each means for energy, mood, and body
  • How estrogen, progesterone, testosterone, and cortisol work in simple, real language
  • What healthy periods actually look like (most women have never been told)
  • The root causes behind the most common cycle symptoms
  • How to eat, move, sleep, and live in harmony with your cycle
  • How to protect your hormones from environmental toxins
  • A complete 30-day beginner plan to start your healing journey today
🌞 My Philosophy
Holistic & Root-Cause

We never chase symptoms. We look for what the body is actually asking for.

We want to go from:
Symptom → take a supplement
to
Symptom → what might be driving it?

Ancestral & Traditional

Women understood their cycles for centuries. We are reclaiming that knowledge.

Nourishment Not Restriction

We feed our hormones. We do not starve them. Food is medicine.

Practical & Compassionate

Every small action you take is a seed planted for your future health. Some days will be better than others, and that is completely okay. This is a journey, so keep showing up for yourself with kindness even when it feels extremely hard.

Natural & Aligned

When you give your body the right food, lifestyle, and environment the ones it was actually designed for it will thrive. Health stops being something you work for and becomes something you simply have.

✓ Beginner Checklist
  • Read through the full guide at least once before making changes
  • Download a cycle tracking app or start a paper journal
  • Note the first day of your last period
  • Begin observing your energy, mood, physical symptoms, and sleep quality across the month
  • Approach this with curiosity, not pressure
Module 02 · Foundation

Why Understanding Your Cycle Changes Everything

Your cycle is not an inconvenience to be managed. It is one of the most intelligent and intricate systems in your body, and chances are, nobody ever taught you how to understand its language.

What to Know
🌺 Your Cycle Is Your Fifth Vital Sign

Doctors measure blood pressure, heart rate, temperature, and respiratory rate as vital signs. The menstrual cycle is now being recognized as the fifth vital sign. When your cycle is irregular, painful, or symptomatic your body is telling you something deeper is off.

"We have normalized period pain for far too long. Painful periods are not genetics or a personality trait. They are a symptom. Suffering every month is not part of being a woman. It is a message from your body."
Why It Matters
  • You stop fighting your body and start working with it
  • You understand why you feel amazing some weeks and exhausted others
  • You can identify root causes of symptoms instead of just suppressing them
  • You feel connected to your body for the first time perhaps ever
  • You finally feel healthy, energized and confident!
The 5 Modern Cycle Disruptors
What Modern Life Did to Women's Hormones
  • Chronic stress and elevated cortisol stealing from progesterone
  • Ultra-processed food and seed oils driving inflammation
  • Synthetic hormones from birth control suppressing the natural cycle
  • Environmental toxins from products and plastics disrupting estrogen
  • Disconnection from natural light, seasonal eating, and body rhythms
Module 03 · The Cycle

The Four Phases of Your Menstrual Cycle

Your cycle is not just your period. It is four completely different seasons each with its own hormones, energy, strengths, and needs.

"Stop looking at your cycle as a burden. It is proof that your body is alive, intelligent, and always working for you. Each phase is a different version of you, equally powerful and equally necessary."

These are averages and will naturally vary from woman to woman

❄
Menstrual Phase
Days 1–5 • Inner Winter
The bleed. Hormones are at their lowest. A time for rest, reflection, and inward focus. Intuition peaks here.
🌿
Follicular Phase
Days 6–13 • Inner Spring
Estrogen rises. Energy returns. You feel clearer, more social, more motivated. Your most fertile creative window.
☀
Ovulation Phase
Around Day 14 • Inner Summer
Estrogen and testosterone peak together. Most magnetic, confident, and communicative. The main event of your cycle.
🍂
Luteal Phase
Days 15–28 • Inner Autumn
Progesterone rises then gradually declines. Energy turns inward and you may feel more reflective and drawn to rest. In the final days, as hormones drop, your body sends natural signals asking for more nourishment and care.

You are not meant to feel exactly the same every single day. Your energy, mood, appetite, and needs naturally shift throughout the month because your hormones are constantly changing. That is your body working exactly as it should.

At the same time, there is a difference between natural fluctuations and unnecessary suffering. While it's normal to experience some shifts throughout your cycle, painful periods, debilitating PMS, extreme fatigue, severe mood swings, and feeling disconnected from your body every month should not be dismissed as "just part of being a woman."

Your cycle is not meant to be a monthly battle. Learn to understand what your body is asking for in each phase so that even in the harder moments, you feel supported, grounded, and at home in yourself.

What Is Actually Happening Biologically
1
Menstruation (Days 1–5)

Estrogen and progesterone fall, and your period begins. This signals the uterus to shed the lining that was built during the previous cycle. At the same time, the brain releases FSH (follicle-stimulating hormone), which begins stimulating a new group of follicles in the ovaries (10–20), each containing an immature egg.

2
Follicular Phase (Days 6–13)

Several follicles continue developing, but one usually becomes dominant and matures an egg fully for ovulation. As it grows, it produces increasing amounts of estrogen, which rebuilds the uterine lining and often supports higher energy, motivation, confidence, and mental clarity.

3
Ovulation (Around Day 14)

Estrogen reaches its peak, triggering a surge of LH (luteinizing hormone). This causes the dominant follicle to release a mature egg from the ovary into the fallopian tube, where it can potentially be fertilized. Testosterone also often peaks around this time, contributing to increased drive, sociability, confidence, and libido.

4
Luteal Phase (Days 15–28)

After releasing the egg, the follicle transforms into the corpus luteum, which produces progesterone. Progesterone becomes the dominant hormone, helping prepare and maintain the uterine lining for a potential pregnancy. If pregnancy does not occur, the corpus luteum breaks down, progesterone and estrogen fall, and a new period begins.

꩜ Your Whole Cycle Is One Flow

Most women blame the luteal phase for everything they feel before their period. But the luteal phase is not the problem. It is simply where everything reveals itself.

From day one of your cycle, every choice you make is either building your body up or drawing it down. If you overtrained during your menstrual phase, if you underate during ovulation, if you ran on stress and poor sleep for weeks, your luteal phase will show you. It holds up a mirror to how you lived the rest of your cycle.

Your cycle is not four separate events. It is one continuous flow. How you nourish yourself in your menstrual phase affects your follicular energy. How you rest during ovulation affects your luteal stability. Every phase feeds the next. When you start caring for your whole cycle, not just the hard days, everything begins to shift.

The luteal phase does not create the problem, it simply reveals how well you have been caring for yourself all along.

Module 04 · Hormones

Hormones Explained Simply

I am not a medical doctor, but I do believe every woman deserves to understand the basics of how her hormones work. More than any scientific term, what matters most is feeling the connection with your own body, testing what works for you, and trusting yourself, because we are all different.

"You are not crazy or broken. You are a woman with a hormonal rhythm that was never explained to you."
~ The Key Hormones
HormoneProduced ByMain RolePeaks
EstrogenOvariesBuilds the uterine lining, supports mood, energy, libido, skin and bone healthLate follicular phase / before ovulation
ProgesteroneCorpus luteumMaintains the uterine lining, promotes calmness and sleep, supports pregnancyMid-luteal phase
TestosteroneOvaries + adrenalsSupports libido, confidence, mental clarity, motivation and driveAround ovulation
FSHPituitary glandStimulates follicle growth and egg maturationEarly follicular phase
LHPituitary glandTriggers ovulation and egg releaseOvulation surge
CortisolAdrenal glandsRegulates energy, blood sugar, wakefulness and stress responseMorning / physical or psychological stress
InsulinPancreasHelps move glucose and nutrients into cells for energy and storageAfter meals

Hormones don't work alone, they're your body's messengers responding to sleep, stress, nutrition, movement, light exposure, and overall health.

⚖ The Most Important Relationships
Estrogen & Progesterone Balance

Too much estrogen relative to progesterone = estrogen dominance. This causes heavy periods, PMS, mood swings, bloating. The liver and gut play a crucial role in clearing excess estrogen.

It's important to understand that even a normal amount of estrogen can become a problem if progesterone is low. It's always about the balance between the two. You want healthy and sufficient levels of both estrogen and progesterone.

Cortisol Steals From Progesterone

When your body is chronically stressed it prioritizes cortisol production. This steals from progesterone because both hormones are made from the same precursor in the body called pregnenolone. When the body must choose between survival (cortisol) and reproduction (progesterone), it always chooses survival. The result is low progesterone and everything that comes with it: worse PMS, poor sleep, anxiety, and irregular cycles.

Things That Can Keep You in Sympathetic Mode (Fight-or-Flight / High Cortisol)

  • 😰 Emotional stress
  • 😴 Poor sleep or sleep deprivation
  • 🍽️ Chronic under-eating or undernourishment
  • 🏃‍♀️ Excessive exercise or overtraining
  • 🤒 Illness
  • 🩹 Injury
  • 🦠 Infection
  • 💔 Major life changes or emotional upheaval
  • 📱 Looking at your phone first thing in the morning
  • ⏰ Rushed, chaotic mornings
  • 💼 Deadlines and work pressure
  • 📺 Constant stimulation while eating (phone, TV, scrolling)
  • 🗣️ Negative relationships, gossip, and draining people
  • ☕ Too much caffeine, especially on an empty stomach
  • ✈️ Travel, jet lag, and disrupted routines
  • 🌙 Staying up late consistently
  • 🚫 Never slowing down or taking time to recover
↳ Analogy

Imagine you are in the wild, being chased by a predator. Your body immediately floods with cortisol, shuts down reproduction, and directs every single resource toward getting you out alive. Progesterone becomes completely irrelevant in that moment. Your body is brilliant. It chose survival.

The problem is that your nervous system cannot tell the difference between a physical danger (being chased by a lion) and an emotional danger (a difficult conversation, a work deadline, financial worry). The stress response fires exactly the same way. And because you are stressed not for a few minutes but chronically, all day every day, your body keeps choosing cortisol over progesterone. Over and over again. This is not normal. Your body was never designed to live in constant stress. And yet here we are.

Insulin Disrupts All Sex Hormones

Blood sugar instability drives insulin spikes which disrupt estrogen, progesterone, and testosterone. Blood sugar balance is foundational for cycle health before any other intervention.

Module 05 · Tracking

How to Track Your Cycle

Tracking your cycle is the single most powerful thing you can do to understand your body. It costs nothing and could truly change a lot.

✎ What to Track Every Day
Physical Signs
  • Period start and end date
  • Flow: light, medium, heavy, clots
  • Cervical mucus changes
  • Basal body temperature (BBT)
  • Bloating or water retention
  • Breast tenderness
  • Skin: clear, breakouts, oily
  • Energy level (1–10) and sleep quality
  • Food cravings
Emotional & Mental Signs
  • Mood overall
  • Anxiety or irritability levels
  • Social desire: people or solitude?
  • Libido
  • Mental clarity or brain fog
  • Motivation and creativity
  • Emotional sensitivity
⚬ Tracking Methods
1
Paper Cycle Diary (Most Intimate)

A small journal noting date, phase, energy, mood, and symptoms daily. The act of writing builds body awareness faster than any app.

2
Clue or Flo App (Easiest Beginner Option)

Free, simple, and good for beginners. Log period dates, symptoms, and mood. The app will begin predicting your phases over time.

3
Basal Body Temperature (Most Accurate)

Take your temperature every morning before getting up with a BBT thermometer. Temperature rises after ovulation this confirms ovulation actually occurred.

4
Cervical Mucus Observation

Dry after period → creamy → watery → egg-white (ovulation) → dry again. Your most honest hormonal indicator. Covered in Module 15.

5
Oura Ring (My Personal Recommendation)

This is an investment but one I truly believe in. The Oura Ring tracks your cycle, your body temperature, your readiness, and your sleep quality all in one place. It can detect subtle temperature shifts that signal where you are in your cycle, often before you even feel it. I personally use it and I can already see my temperature rising, my readiness dropping, sometimes my sleep shifting, and I know my period is coming. It connects you to your body in a way that is hard to explain until you experience it. If you can invest in one thing for your cycle health, this would be it.

Module 06 · Cycle Health

Signs of a Healthy Cycle

Most women have never been told what a healthy period actually looks like. This is the conversation your doctor should have had with you years ago.

🌸 Signs Your Cycle Is Healthy
  • Cycle length of 24–35 days
  • Period lasting 3–7 days
  • Bright red blood for most of the bleed (think cranberry juice)
  • Minimal to no cramping (mild discomfort is completely normal, but pain that stops your day is not)
  • Light to moderate flow
  • Minimal clots (some small clots are completely normal, but very large or very frequent clots are worth paying attention to)
  • Ovulation occurring consistently (confirmed by a BBT rise, fertile cervical mucus changes)
  • Noticeable cervical mucus changes throughout the cycle (dry → creamy → watery → egg-white → drier again)
  • A clear temperature shift after ovulation (showing progesterone is being produced)
  • Consistent energy and stable moods throughout most of the month
  • Feeling hungry at appropriate times and having healthy appetite signals
  • Waking up with energy and recovering well from exercise
  • Feeling well in your body rather than constantly fighting symptoms
  • Your libido naturally fluctuates throughout the cycle, with a noticeable increase in late follicular phase (around ovulation)
  • Minimal PMS (some mild mood shifts are normal, but debilitating symptoms are not)
  • Your period arrives predictably, around the same time each cycle (and if it is ever late, you can usually trace it back to something, poor sleep, high stress, travel, a big move, or simply a lot going on)
  • Your period feels like a transition, not a crisis

❀ The goal is to feel connected to your body's signals.

🩸 Understanding Your Blood Color

Your period blood is a monthly message from your body. Learn to read yours.

ColorWhat It May Mean
Bright red (cherry red, wine red, or cranberry red)Healthy, fresh blood flowing well. This is what you are aiming for.
Dark redGenerally normal, especially on the first day when flow is heavier. If consistently dark red or very dark red throughout your period, it may be a sign of high estrogen or estrogen dominance. It may be worth supporting your liver, gut, and circulation.
Brown or dark brownOlder blood that oxidized before leaving the uterus. Very common at the very start or end of your period and not a concern in small amounts. It can be a sign of low progesterone or that you may not be keeping your uterus warm enough.
Pink or very lightCould be a sign of low estrogen due to under-eating, over-exercising, or chronic stress. Could also be a sign of low iron. Worth paying attention to if it happens consistently.
OrangeCan be normal when period blood mixes with cervical fluid, but could also indicate an infection. Worth watching if accompanied by unusual odor, itching, or discomfort. If those symptoms are present → Speak with a practitioner.
GrayGray is not a normal color variation and can indicate bacterial vaginosis or another infection, especially if accompanied by odor or discomfort → Speak with a practitioner.
☀ A Simple Tip for Keeping Your Uterus Warm

If you often see brown or dark blood, or experience cramping, one of the simplest and most overlooked things you can do is keep your feet warm. Cold feet mean cold circulation, and poor circulation means your uterus is not receiving the warmth and blood flow it needs. Wearing socks, especially during your period, is a small but genuinely effective way to support uterine warmth.

⚠ Signs Worth Addressing
  • Cycles consistently shorter than 21 days or longer than 35 days
  • Periods lasting longer than 7 days or shorter than 2 days
  • Heavy bleeding that soaks a pad every hour for several hours
  • Severe cramping that requires medication every cycle
  • Absent periods for 3+ months outside of pregnancy
  • Extreme PMS, rage, or mood disorder before your period
  • Pain during intercourse
  • No obvious signs of ovulation
  • Spotting before or between periods
Important Note

These signs are invitations to explore root causes. This guide gives you tools to support your cycle naturally, but always work with a trusted practitioner for persistent or serious symptoms.

Module 07 · Root Causes

Common Symptoms & What They May Indicate

Your symptoms are not random. They are your body's language and once you learn to read it, everything changes.

"Your symptoms are not your enemy. They are your body speaking in the only language it has."
Why Some Women Have Severe PMS and Others Do Not

Progesterone drops before every period for every woman. So why do some feel completely fine while others feel debilitated? It is not just about the hormones themselves. It is about how well the body can handle those shifts. A woman whose body is well nourished, well rested, with a healthy liver, balanced gut, stable blood sugar, and a regulated nervous system will feel the same hormonal drop very differently from a woman whose body is depleted, inflamed, and running on stress.

The Real Root Causes of Severe PMS
  • Magnesium deficiency Research shows up to 70% of women with PMS are deficient in magnesium. Without enough magnesium the nervous system cannot buffer the hormonal drop smoothly, making anxiety, cramps, mood swings, and insomnia significantly worse.
  • Blood sugar instability When blood sugar is unstable all month the nervous system is already under stress. Adding a progesterone drop on top makes everything feel far more intense. Stable blood sugar alone dramatically reduces PMS severity for many women.
  • Sluggish liver When the liver cannot clear estrogen efficiently, estrogen stays elevated relative to falling progesterone. This imbalance amplifies every PMS symptom including bloating, breast tenderness, and mood disruption.
  • Poor gut health The estrobolome, the community of gut bacteria responsible for clearing estrogen, plays a direct role. When gut health is compromised, estrogen recirculates instead of being excreted, worsening the hormonal imbalance in the days before your period.
  • Chronic stress Cortisol steals from progesterone all month long. By the time the luteal drop happens, progesterone was already low to begin with, so the crash hits from a much lower starting point and feels far harder.
  • Inflammation A diet high in seed oils, refined sugar, and processed food amplifies prostaglandin production which directly drives stronger uterine contractions, more cramping, more pain, and more bloating.
  • Dysregulated nervous system Women running on chronic stress or living in a constant state of fight or flight feel hormonal shifts much more intensely. Supporting nervous system regulation is one of the most overlooked and most powerful tools for genuine PMS relief.

PMS is not caused by your hormones changing. Every woman's hormones change. It is often caused by a body that is under-supported, undernourished, overstressed, or lacking the resources needed to support healthy hormone function. And that is something you can absolutely work on.

⚬ Symptom Root Cause Guide
SymptomPossible Root CauseWhere to Start
Painful crampsHigh prostaglandins, inflammation, low magnesiumAnti-inflammatory diet (ginger, tart cherries, extra virgin olive oil, fatty fish), magnesium, liver support
Heavy bleedingEstrogen dominance, low progesteroneLiver support, reduce estrogen load, gut health, iron-rich foods like pomegranate and dark leafy greens
PMS mood swingsLow magnesium, low serotonin, blood sugar instability, chronically low progesterone from stress, or excess estrogen from poor liver clearanceBlood sugar balance, magnesium, stress reduction, liver support, anti-inflammatory foods like rosemary and wild blueberries
Bloating pre-periodHigh progesterone, water retention, gut dysbiosisGut health, hydration, reduce processed food, gut-calming foods like purslane and ginger
Breakouts pre-periodEstrogen and progesterone ratio shift, gut-liver axis imbalanceLiver support, gut health, reduce sugar, sea buckthorn for its skin-calming antioxidants
Fatigue across cycleIron deficiency, thyroid, adrenal fatigueNutrient-dense eating, iron-rich foods like dark leafy greens and pomegranate, omega-3s from fatty fish, stress support
Irregular cyclesChronic stress, under-eating, PCOS, thyroidNourishment, stress reduction, cycle tracking
Low libidoLow testosterone, pill suppression, chronic stressAdrenal support, reduce stress, assess pill use
Sugar cravings pre-periodLow serotonin, low magnesium, blood sugar instability. Some cravings are completely normal as your metabolism increases before your period.Reach for natural sugar sources, have structured meals, and eat whole foods. Avoid grazing all day and processed snacks.
The Three Foundations Before Everything Else
◦ Liver Health

The liver clears excess estrogen. When overloaded, estrogen recirculates and drives most cycle symptoms. Support with bitter foods, cruciferous vegetables, adequate protein, and reducing your toxic load (this includes natural cosmetic products, shampoos, fabrics/clothing, cookware and cleaning products free from hormone-disrupting chemicals).

🦠 Gut Health

Your gut is your second brain and is connected to everything. Even if you have no obvious cycle problems, a healthy gut is the foundation of good health. The estrobolome, gut bacteria responsible for estrogen metabolism, plays a direct role in your cycle. When gut health is poor, estrogen recirculates instead of being excreted. Support with fermented foods and fiber.

☼ Adrenal & Nervous System Health

The adrenals produce cortisol which steals from progesterone under chronic stress. Supporting your nervous system is supporting your hormones.

Module 08 · Nourishment

Nutrition for Each Cycle Phase

What you eat is either feeding your hormones or fighting them. Food is the most powerful lever you have and it costs less than your birth control prescription.

Foundation Principles Every Phase
Prioritize Always
  • Fresh seasonal fruit
  • Grass-fed, ethically raised meat (beef, lamb, bison, venison)
  • Raw dairy where available (raw milk, raw cheese, raw butter, kefir) or full-fat organic as an alternative
  • Pasture-raised eggs
  • Cold pressed oils (extra virgin olive oil, coconut oil, ghee, avocado oil)
  • Local root vegetables and vegetables in season
  • Coconut water and fresh pressed juices
  • Sourdough or fermented bread if eating bread
  • Clean filtered or distilled water. Optional: a water vortexer to energize your water.
  • Complex carbs such as organic white or jasmine rice, quinoa, and lentils

Food matters deeply, but quality and sourcing matter even more. Always aim to know where your food comes from.

Arrange your food intake around how physically active you are. For example, if you are not very active, you do not need large amounts of carbohydrates. Vegetables and fruit are enough of a carbohydrate source for most people. Aim for a balanced diet of natural, whole foods and listen to your body for what works and what doesn't.

⚠ Avoid Always
  • Seed oils
  • Ultra-processed foods
  • Refined sugar especially pre-period
  • Plastic products
  • Tap water
  • Conventional beauty, body and hair care products, especially those with synthetic fragrances
  • Conventional plastic period products
  • Under-eating or over-training
  • Alcohol
  • Smoking
🌸 Menstrual Phase ~ Replenish & Restore
FocusWhyFoods
Iron-rich foodsReplace iron lost in bloodGrass-fed beef, lamb, liver, dark leafy greens, lentils, pumpkin seeds
MagnesiumReduces cramping and inflammationDark chocolate, cacao, coconut water, avocado
Warm, cooked foodsEasier on digestion, warming and comfortingSoups (butternut squash soup), stews, roasted root vegetables, bone broth
Omega-3 fatsReduce prostaglandins (cramping chemicals)Salmon, sardines, walnuts, flaxseeds (soaked or sprouted)
Ginger teaNatural prostaglandin inhibitorFresh ginger root tea, 2–3 cups daily
🍇 Follicular Phase ~ Build & Energize
FocusWhyFoods
Liver-supporting foodsSupport estrogen metabolism as it risesSwiss chard, bitter herbs, lemon water, artichokes, liver-stimulating herbs, olive oil
ZincSupports follicle developmentPumpkin seeds, red meat, oysters, eggs
Fermented foodsSupport gut health and estrobolomeRaw yogurt, kefir, sauerkraut, raw cheese
Fresh raw fruitDigestion is at its strongest in this phase. Raw fruit will help you feel even better.Seasonal fruits (watermelon, berries, citrus fruit), cucumber, fresh herbs, avocado
☀ Ovulation Phase ~ Sustain Your Peak
FocusWhyFoods
Zinc-rich foodsCritical for healthy ovulation and LH surgeOysters, pumpkin seeds, red meat, eggs
AntioxidantsProtect the egg from oxidative stressBerries, cranberries, pomegranate, fresh herbs and spices, olive oil
Cruciferous vegetablesHelp the liver bind and clear excess estrogen through the gutBroccoli, cauliflower, Brussels sprouts, cabbage
HydrationCervical mucus production needs adequate hydrationCoconut water, fresh juices, mineral water
🍂 Luteal Phase ~ Ground & Support
FocusWhyFoods
MagnesiumSupports progesterone, reduces PMS, calms nervous systemDark chocolate 85%+, pumpkin seeds, leafy greens
B6 vitaminSupports progesterone production and moodBananas, potatoes, sunflower seeds, beef liver, eggs, wild salmon
Complex carbohydratesStabilize blood sugar as progesterone affects insulin sensitivitySeasonal fruit, root vegetables, sweet potato, white rice, quinoa, wild rice, butternut squash
Calcium-rich foodsReduces PMS symptomsRaw dairy, sardines with bones, leafy greens, sesame
Warming soups and stewsDigestion slows in luteal, cooked foods are easier and more nourishing. Sulfur-rich vegetables are especially supportive eaten as soups.Bone broth, vegetable broth, butternut squash soup, meat stews, roasted root vegetables

A note on grains: I am personally not a fan of making grains the center of your diet. Seasonal fruit and root vegetables are your best carb sources. White rice is fine occasionally but keep it as a side, not a staple.

⚬ Seed Cycling (Optional)

Seed cycling is a holistic practice of eating specific seeds during each half of your cycle to gently support hormone balance. It works best as a complementary practice, not a cure. Some women find it very helpful, others notice little difference. Try it for at least 3 cycles before deciding if it works for you.

Important: when eating any nuts or seeds, always soak and or sprout them first, or at minimum rinse them well. This removes phytic acid and makes them far more digestible and nutritious. The same applies to grains. I personally am not a fan of grains and prefer an ancestral approach centered around seasonal fruit, quality animal foods, and root vegetables. Keep it simple.

Follicular & Ovulation Phase (Days 1–14)
  • Flaxseeds (soaked or ground) ~ support estrogen metabolism
  • Pumpkin seeds (soaked) ~ rich in zinc to support ovulation

How to use: add to smoothies, sprinkle on fruit, stir into raw yogurt, or add to soups

Luteal & Menstrual Phase (Days 15–28)
  • Sesame seeds (soaked or tahini) ~ support progesterone
  • Sunflower seeds (soaked) ~ rich in vitamin E and selenium

How to use: tahini in dressings, seeds in soups, stirred into warm broths, or added to roasted vegetables

Pay attention to how your body responds to different foods, and prioritize those that support smooth digestion, satisfaction, full energy, and make you feel your best!

Module 09 · Movement

Movement for Each Cycle Phase

Training the same way every day of the month is like planting seeds in winter and wondering why nothing grows.

"Your body is not a machine to be optimized the same way every day. It is a living, rhythmic system that deserves to be moved in harmony with its season."
❄
Menstrual
Rest & Gentle Movement
Walking, gentle yoga, stretching, swimming slowly. Honor the inward pull. Rest is productive here, your body is working hard.
🌿
Follicular
Build & Explore
Increase intensity gradually. Strength training, pilates, cycling, yoga flows. Estrogen supports muscle building and recovery.
☀
Ovulation
Peak Performance
Your highest energy and pain tolerance. Dancing, group fitness, HIIT, lifting.
🍂
Luteal
Maintain & Wind Down
Moderate strength training early, then shift to walks, yoga, and stretching as you approach your period. Avoid exhausting yourself.
⚬ A Personal Note on Movement

Every body is different, but women are very easy to overtrain. We have been taught to push hard, train intensely, and go to the gym and stress ourselves out, when in reality these kinds of workouts are often hurting us more than helping. High intensity training is genuinely stressful for the female body and can disrupt hormones, suppress ovulation, and drain your energy reserves over time.

What I truly believe in is natural, gentle, joyful movement. My favorites are walking, swimming, strength training, and cycling in nature. These activities give you daily sunlight, connect you to the outdoors, move your body without excessive stress, and support your hormonal health far more than a high intensity gym session ever could.

Move your body in ways that feel good. Connect with nature. Get sunlight. That is your best workout.

The Most Underrated Movement for Women
Walking Your Hormones' Best Friend

1 hour of daily walking in natural light reduces cortisol, improves insulin sensitivity, supports lymphatic drainage, and boosts mood. Aim for 10,000 steps every day. It is the single best movement practice for hormonal health across all phases and most women underestimate it completely.

💧 The Importance of Sweating

Sweating is one of your body's natural ways to support detoxification, circulation, and overall hormone health, so do not underestimate it. In the summer, this comes easily. You can sweat just sitting outside in the heat, and moving your body during these months makes the benefits even greater. In the winter, when sweating naturally happens less, you can recreate those benefits through saunas and slightly more intense movement to support efficient detoxification. Hiking, strength training, rebounding, cycling, stair climbing, dancing, or even a long, fast-paced walk can all work up a good sweat and keep this important detox pathway active all year round (movement can absolutely be enjoyable!).

💫 Morning Lymphatic Stimulation

Your lymphatic system has no pump of its own, it relies entirely on movement to flow properly. Starting your morning with a few minutes of lymphatic stimulation helps clear stagnation overnight, reduces puffiness and bloating, supports detoxification, and gets your circulation going before the rest of your day begins.

Try a few of these each morning:

  • Jumping in place and rebounding (mini trampoline) ~ two of the best ways to get the lymph moving quickly
  • Walking
  • Massaging around your lymph nodes (neck, underarms, groin)
  • Body and face dry brushing
  • Face gua sha
⚠ When Exercise Hurts Your Hormones
  • Training intensely every day regardless of phase: cortisol accumulation suppresses ovulation
  • Fasted intense exercise: spikes cortisol and adrenaline, disrupts blood sugar
  • Over-exercising while under-eating: signals famine to the body, suppresses reproductive hormones, lowers metabolism, and encourages fat storage
  • Ignoring fatigue in the luteal and menstrual phases

One of the most misunderstood topics in women's health is exercise. Many women believe that more intensity always means better results, that you need to push through exhaustion, sweat through every session, and grind with extreme cardio to be healthy. But your body does not work that way. Gentle, consistent movement like gardening, walking, yoga, and stretching can support your hormones, nervous system, and energy levels far more than constant high-intensity training.

Module 10 · Nervous System

Stress, Nervous System & Hormones

You cannot out-supplement a chronically stressed nervous system. Stress is not just a feeling, it is a hormonal event that disrupts your cycle.

The Cortisol Connection

When you are chronically stressed, your adrenal glands produce cortisol continuously. Cortisol and progesterone compete for the same precursor pregnenolone. When the body must choose between survival (cortisol) and reproduction (progesterone), it chooses survival every time. Chronic stress literally steals your progesterone.

Low Progesterone Signs Driven by Stress
  • Severe PMS: mood swings, anxiety, irritability before period
  • Insomnia in the week before your period
  • Short luteal phase (less than 10 days)
  • Spotting before period starts
  • Irregular or absent cycles
☼ Nervous System Support Practices
1
Morning Sunlight (Free & Powerful)

10–20 minutes of morning sunlight within one hour of waking regulates cortisol, sets your circadian rhythm, supports serotonin, and improves sleep all directly benefiting your cycle.

2
Breathwork

Deep intentional breathing activates the parasympathetic nervous system within minutes. You do not need a specific method. Simply breathe deeply 5 to 10 times, slowly and with intention. Do this before meals, before sleep, or any time stress rises.

3
Nature and Earthing

Walking barefoot on grass, sitting near water, spending time in nature lowers cortisol measurably. Our ancestors lived in constant contact with the earth, and our bodies still respond to it deeply.

4
Boundary Setting as Hormone Health

Saying no, protecting your time, and creating space for rest is not selfish, it is necessary for your hormones. Chronic people-pleasing is a real stressor with real hormonal consequences.

5
Herbal Adaptogens

Certain herbs such as ashwagandha and maca can gently support adrenal function and help the body adapt to stress over time. Always prioritize food first, and if you choose to supplement, research quality brands carefully.

6
A 2-Minute Vagus Nerve Practice

This is one of the simplest and most effective things you can do to quickly calm your nervous system. Place both hands behind your head, interlacing your fingers so your elbows point out to the sides. Take a breath, and then slowly move only your eyes to the left, holding for 10 to 15 seconds. Then move your eyes slowly to the right, holding again for 10 to 15 seconds. Breathe naturally throughout. You may feel a sigh, a swallow, or a wave of calm. That is your vagus nerve activating, shifting your body from fight or flight into rest and digest. The vagus nerve is the main nerve of the parasympathetic nervous system, and this simple eye movement practice is one of the fastest ways to access it.

7
Prayer and Bible Reading

Spending time in prayer and reading the Bible can bring deep peace, perspective, and a sense of being grounded and cared for. For many women, this practice calms anxious thoughts and provides genuine comfort to the nervous system, supporting overall stress resilience.

Additional practices: journaling, spending time with people you love, laughing, gentle movement, playing an instrument, and meditation (incredible practice). Do not underestimate these.

Module 11 · Sleep

Sleep & Recovery

Sleep is when your hormones are made, repaired, and balanced. Poor sleep is one of the most underrated drivers of hormonal dysfunction in women.

What Happens to Hormones During Sleep
  • Growth hormone is released in the first 2 hours: repairs tissue and supports metabolism
  • Cortisol drops to its lowest point between midnight and 4am, allowing the nervous system to repair
  • Progesterone supports deep sleep via GABA receptors: this is why progesterone deficiency causes sleep difficulties
  • Melatonin protects eggs from oxidative damage, directly supporting fertility and cycle quality
  • Leptin and ghrelin (appetite hormones) are regulated during sleep: poor sleep drives sugar and carb cravings
☽ Sleep Across the Cycle
Menstrual Phase

You may need 1–2 extra hours. Honor this, your body is working hard. Most important phase for deep, restorative sleep.

Follicular Phase

Sleep quality improves as estrogen rises. You naturally need slightly less sleep and wake with more energy.

Ovulatory Phase

Sleep is typically best here. Estrogen and testosterone are high, body temperature is stable.

Luteal Phase

The most disrupted sleep phase. As progesterone drops in late luteal, sleep becomes fragmented and you may wake during the night. This is the phase where sleep deserves the most intention. Prioritize an early bedtime, eat magnesium-rich foods throughout the day (dark chocolate, pumpkin seeds, leafy greens), and take magnesium glycinate before bed. It is transformative.

If you aim for 7–10 hours of quality sleep (in bed from 8–10pm) every day, you will have healthy hormones!

☾ Sleep Foundations
1
Sleep in Complete Darkness

Even small amounts of light suppress melatonin. Blackout curtains or a sleep mask are non-negotiable for hormonal recovery.

2
Consistent Sleep Schedule

Going to bed and waking at the same time every day regulates cortisol, supports circadian rhythm, and improves all downstream hormones.

3
Eat Early in the Day

Try to have your last meal at least 2 to 4 hours before bed. Eating late burdens digestion and raises body temperature, both of which disrupt sleep quality. My personal recommendation is to stop eating around 5pm. It sounds early but the difference in sleep quality is significant.

4
Morning and Evening Sun

Getting sunlight in the morning sets your cortisol rhythm and signals your body to wake up. Getting softer light in the evening, ideally from the sunset, signals your body that night is coming and supports natural melatonin production. Both are free and profoundly effective for sleep quality.

5
Avoid Screens Before Bed

Blue light suppresses melatonin and delays sleep onset. Try to avoid screens a few hours before bed, or wear red light glasses if you need to use your phone or computer in the evening. Particularly impactful in the luteal phase when sleep is already fragile.

6
Sleep in a Cooler Bedroom 17–19°C (63–66°F)

Your body temperature naturally drops about 1 degree as you fall asleep, and a cooler room makes this transition far easier, supporting deeper, more restorative sleep. For most of human history, there was no thermostat. Our ancestors slept outdoors or in simple shelters, fully exposed to the natural drop in temperature that happens every night after the sun sets. Our bodies evolved around that cooling cycle, it is part of what tells your nervous system it is safe to rest.

  • The sun went down → darkness signaled sleep
  • The air temperature dropped → helping the body cool down
  • People often slept in simple shelters, tents, huts, or outdoors
  • There was more airflow and ventilation
  • There were no heated mattresses, electronics, or insulated bedrooms trapping heat

Today, overheated bedrooms work against this natural rhythm. Keeping your room cool, cracking a window, or lowering the thermostat in the evening is one of the simplest ways to recreate the environment your body was actually designed to sleep in.

Optional: Magnesium glycinate (200–400mg) before bed is a well-researched natural support for sleep, especially during the luteal phase when progesterone drops. It activates GABA receptors, the same calming pathway progesterone uses.

Module 12 · Blood Sugar

Blood Sugar & Women's Hormones

Before addressing any specific hormone, every woman should stabilize her blood sugar. It is the foundation everything else is built on.

Why Blood Sugar Matters

Every time you eat refined carbohydrates or sugar, your blood sugar spikes rapidly. Your pancreas releases insulin to bring it back down. This insulin spike disrupts the delicate signaling between your brain and your ovaries affecting FSH, LH, estrogen, and progesterone production.

Blood Sugar Instability Drives
  • Worsened PMS and mood swings
  • Increased androgens (PCOS-like symptoms, acne, hair loss)
  • Irregular cycles insulin affects LH pulsing
  • Weight gain around the middle and hips
  • Intensified sugar cravings before your period
  • Energy crashes and fatigue throughout the cycle
Blood Sugar Balancing for Women
1
Structured Meal Eating

Eat your meals at or around the same time every day. Do not snack constantly or graze in between meals. Give your body proper meals and proper rest between them. This alone significantly improves insulin sensitivity and blood sugar regulation.

2
Walk After Meals

Even a short walk after eating has been shown in multiple studies to significantly reduce post-meal blood sugar spikes. Your muscles absorb glucose directly during movement. Do not sit all day. Include gentle movement throughout your day, and especially after meals.

3
Eat the Majority of Your Calories Earlier in the Day

Consuming most of your food in the first half of the day is one of the most powerful things you can do for blood sugar, metabolism, and sleep. My personal recommendation is to stop eating between 4 and 7pm, with your last meal ideally around 5pm. It takes adjustment, but the results are significant.

4
Eliminate Refined/Processed Foods

Refined sugar, processed carbohydrates, fast food, and highly altered foods (low-fat products, protein bars, sweetened drinks, ready-made meals and packaged snacks) spike insulin rapidly and repeatedly, driving hormonal imbalance and destroying gut health over time. The first and most important thing is to start making as much food at home from whole foods. A few simple swaps you can make are switching regular bread for sourdough and white pasta for buckwheat pasta (and if you eat white pasta, make it at home and cook it al dente!).

5
Nourish Your Body Enough

Under-eating is a blood sugar stressor. Skipping meals spikes cortisol and disrupts insulin sensitivity. Nourish your body enough throughout the day, especially in the luteal phase when your needs genuinely increase.

6
Switch to Natural Sugar Sources

Instead of refined sugar, reach for natural sources your body actually recognizes: raw honey, maple syrup, seasonal and local fruit, and dates occasionally. These are the only sweeteners your body truly needs.

7
Do Not Build Meals Around Carbs

Most people consume far more carbohydrates than our bodies were ancestrally designed for, often without even realizing it, because vegetables and fruit alone already provide a meaningful amount. Instead of building your plate around carbs, prioritize your other macronutrients: protein, healthy fats, sugar from fruit, and root vegetables. You can absolutely include some white rice, quinoa, or sourdough now and then, just do not make it the staple of every single meal. That said, if you exercise a lot, your carbohydrate needs increase, and that is completely fine. You always have to accommodate your eating to your lifestyle.

Module 13 · Hydration

Hydration & Mineral Balance

Your hydration needs change across your cycle. Plain water is not always enough and being bloated does not mean you are hydrated.

"Puffy does not mean hydrated. Your cells can be thirsty while your body is holding on to stagnant fluid."
∿ Hydration by Phase
PhaseWhat's HappeningHydration Strategy
MenstrualBlood loss, hormone crash, electrolytes depletedFiltered water with real salt, filtered water with lemon, coconut water, ginger/raspberry leaf/chamomile teas, bone broth
FollicularEstrogen rising, sodium excretion higherIncrease water intake, mineral-rich water, fresh juices, hydrating fruits
OvulationCervical mucus production requires hydrationIncrease water + electrolytes, coconut water, hydrating fruits and vegetables
LutealProgesterone causes water retention, cells still dehydratedDrink MORE clean water + electrolytes to flush retained fluid. Reduce processed sodium.
The Mineral Trio
What Your Cycle Needs
  • Magnesium depleted fastest during luteal and menstrual phases. Dark chocolate, pumpkin seeds, leafy greens, magnesium glycinate supplement
  • Potassium regulates fluid balance and reduces bloating. Coconut water, bananas, avocado, sweet potato
  • Real Sodium ~ Use quality unrefined salts: Himalayan pink salt, Celtic grey sea salt, Kosher crystal salt, Jacobsen salt, Maldon crystal salt. I personally use flower of sea salt from the island of Pag in Croatia. Stay away from bleached, iodized table salt.
🌊 The Truth About Salt in Your Water

Adding a pinch of salt to water became a huge trend, and while electrolytes genuinely matter, this is one of the most misunderstood pieces of health advice out there. A little goes a long way, and honestly, you do not need to be salting your water every single day. It is a bit of a misconception. It is also worth saying clearly: real, unrefined salt is good for you, but bleached, iodized table salt is never a good choice.

For example, I live in Croatia and swim in the sea regularly. Just from that alone, my skin and body absorb plenty of natural minerals and salt. On top of that, most people already salt their food while cooking and at the table. Add salted water on top of all that, and it quickly becomes too much. Real hydration comes from filtered water, electrolyte-rich natural drinks (coconut water), real living food (especially fruit), and water infused with herbs, fruit, or some lemon juice, not from constantly adding sodium to everything you drink.

Historically, salt was actually quite rare and expensive. For most of human history, people ate far less sodium than we do today, salt had to be mined, traded, or evaporated from seawater, and it was treated as a valuable, limited resource. The word "salary" actually comes from the Latin word salarium, linked to salt, a highly valued commodity in ancient Rome. Diets were naturally lower in sodium and far higher in potassium from fresh fruits and vegetables. Our modern food environment has flipped this completely, with sodium hidden in nearly every processed product.

Do not get me wrong, sodium is essential. But like most things, it is about not overdoing it. And again, there is no single intake that fits everyone. It hugely depends on your activity level and how much you sweat throughout the day. Listen to your body, do not overdo sodium, and let real, whole foods do most of the work.

🍵 The Three Teas for Your Cycle
Ginger Tea For Cramps

Contains gingerols that inhibit prostaglandins the same mechanism as ibuprofen. Multiple clinical trials show ginger is as effective as mefenamic acid for period pain. 2–3 cups daily during menstruation. Start one day before your period for best results.

Red Raspberry Leaf Tea For Your Uterus

The most traditional women's herb. Tones and relaxes uterine muscles, reduces cramping and heavy bleeding over time. Best used consistently throughout the cycle not just during your period.

Chamomile Tea For Your Nervous System

Reduces period pain, calms the nervous system, supports sleep in the luteal phase. Studies comparing chamomile with NSAIDs found comparable results for period pain relief.

"One tea for your cramps. One for your uterus. One for your nervous system."

Module 14 · Clean Living

Environmental Toxins & Hormonal Health

You cannot out-eat a toxic environment. Endocrine disruptors in everyday products are silently interfering with your hormones and most women have no idea.

What Are Endocrine Disruptors?

Endocrine disruptors are chemicals that mimic, block, or interfere with your hormones particularly estrogen. They enter through skin, food, water, and air. They accumulate over time and contribute to estrogen dominance, thyroid issues, fertility problems, and cycle disruption.

The Most Common Sources
  • Plastic food containers and water bottles (BPA, phthalates)
  • Conventional beauty and skincare products (parabens, synthetic fragrance)
  • Commercial deodorants (aluminum, synthetic fragrance)
  • Conventional cleaning products (synthetic fragrances, chlorine)
  • Pesticides on non-organic produce (glyphosate especially)
  • Conventional laundry detergents and fabric softeners
  • Non-stick cookware (PFAS forever chemicals)
The Clean Swap Priority List
1
Swap Plastic for Glass and Stainless Steel

Never heat food in plastic. Store food in glass containers. Use a stainless steel or glass water bottle. This single swap reduces daily endocrine disruptor exposure significantly.

2
Clean Up Your Skincare, Makeup and Deodorant

Your skin is your largest organ, what goes on it goes in you. Avoid parabens, synthetic fragrance (listed as "parfum"), aluminum, and PEGs. Look for certified organic products for everything you put on your skin, including makeup.

3
Switch to Natural Period Products

Conventional tampons and pads contain pesticide residues, bleach, and synthetic fragrance in direct contact with highly absorptive tissue. Organic cotton pads or period underwear are a cleaner option. I am personally not a fan of tampons. I believe the natural flow of menstrual blood is exactly that, natural, and interrupting it by holding it inside the body is not something I feel aligned with. This is simply my own perspective, and you are always welcome to do your own research and choose what feels right for you.

4
Natural Laundry & Cleaning

White distilled vinegar as fabric softener, baking soda for cleaning power, tea tree oil for antimicrobial properties and for killing dust mites, bed bugs, and bacteria especially important if you have pets. These replace almost every commercial cleaning product effectively and without the hormonal cost. Also look for a natural laundry detergent. I personally buy mine from a small shop in Croatia, made with lavender essential oil, and I love it.

5
Filter Your Water

Tap water contains traces of hormones, pharmaceuticals, chlorine, and heavy metals. A quality filter (reverse osmosis or activated carbon) is one of the best investments for long-term hormonal health.

6
Upgrade Your Cookware and Kitchen Tools

What you cook with matters just as much as what you cook. Swap non-stick or Teflon pans for stainless steel or cast iron, both heat beautifully and will not leach PFAS into your food. Switch out plastic sponges for stainless steel scrubbers, natural wood brushes, or bamboo dish brushes, swap synthetic cleaning rags for linen cleaning cloths, switch the utensils and spoons you eat from for wooden or stainless steel versions, and choose ceramic plates and bowls over plastic ones, or eat straight off a wooden board, honestly one of my favorite ways to eat. These small kitchen upgrades add up to a meaningfully lower toxic load, meal after meal.

7
Swap Your Toilet Paper

Most conventional toilet paper is bleached and treated with chemical processing agents, residues that come into direct contact with extremely sensitive, absorptive tissue. Switching to unbleached bamboo toilet paper, or even better, using a bidet and cleaning with water, is a simple, low-effort upgrade that removes this exposure completely. I cover this in much more depth, along with two other surprising swaps, in my YouTube video "3 Things Sabotaging Your Hormones Before 9AM (What I Use Now)", definitely worth a watch.

For detailed product swaps and brand recommendations, visit my YouTube channel, Anja Gregoran, and watch "I Swapped These 9 Things and My Hormones Finally Balanced." In it, I share the exact products I use and recommend.

You'll also find some of my favorite brands and product recommendations in the final module of this course.

And if you'd like to continue learning alongside a community of like-minded women, join my Skool community, Naturally with Anja. It's a space where we share tips, experiences, and inspiration, learn from one another, and support each other on the journey to better health.

As a member, you'll also receive Modus Vivendi: Timeless Lessons from The Adriatic, where I share simple lessons inspired by the Mediterranean lifestyle, from nourishing foods and healthy habits to a slower, more balanced way of living.

⚬ YouTube: Anja Gregoran
⚬ Skool: Naturally with Anja

Module 15 · Fertility Awareness

Fertility Awareness Basics

Whether or not you want to conceive right now, understanding your fertility signs is the most empowering thing you can do for your body literacy.

🌺 So many women put off or underestimate the impact of swapping their products, eliminating harmful makeup, getting restful sleep, or prioritizing whole foods, until they finally realize that none of this is optional if they want their hormones to actually function. Years of overload on the body, through toxins, stress, and poor nourishment, can genuinely contribute to fertility struggles. A woman's nutrition, health, lifestyle, and overall well-being before and during pregnancy can influence fertility, pregnancy outcomes, and child development (the environment a baby develops in absolutely matters). Here is what I want you to take from this: take care of your body, and most importantly, never think you are cursed or simply unable to have a child. Your body is responding to its environment, and environments can change. I have seen this firsthand. A family member of my boyfriend and his wife struggled for a long time to conceive. After fasting for 30 days together in prayer, they finally got pregnant. This reflects something powerful: the body has an INCREDIBLE capacity to heal and align once we remove what is interfering and give it the right conditions, support, and faith. I share this not to say that prolonged fasting is the only way or to create fear, but to empower you to make healthy decisions every day and to always know that your body is SO SO powerful! I mean, after all, it is GOD'S DESIGN!

"Knowing your body's fertile signs is not just about pregnancy. It is about truly understanding one of the most beautiful processes your body goes through every single month. Ovulation is the main event of your cycle, and learning to recognize it is one of the most empowering things you can do for your health."
The Two Primary Signs
Cervical Mucus Changes
PhaseMucus AppearanceWhat It Means
After periodDry or nothingLow estrogen, not fertile
FollicularSticky, white, crumblyEstrogen rising, approaching fertility
Pre-ovulationCreamy, lotion-likeFertile estrogen high
OvulationClear, stretchy, egg-whitePeak fertility ovulation imminent
Post-ovulationDry againProgesterone dominant, not fertile
Basal Body Temperature (BBT)

Your resting temperature is lower in the follicular phase and rises by 0.2–0.5 degrees after ovulation due to progesterone. Taking your temperature every morning before getting up and charting it confirms ovulation occurred. Ovulation is not important just for fertility, but also for healthy libido and your natural hormone production. Without ovulation, none of these are fully supported.

💊 Birth Control & Your Natural Cycle

If you are on the pill, you are not ovulating and therefore not producing your natural hormones. Your cervical mucus is suppressed. Your BBT is flat.

The pill comes with a range of side effects and health consequences that are deeply underreported, including changes in mood, libido, gut health, nutrient depletion, and long-term hormonal disruption. Many women experience a version of themselves on the pill that feels flat, disconnected, and unlike who they truly are, without ever connecting it to the pill itself.

If you are currently on the pill and it resonates with you to come off it, I would encourage you to do so slowly and with proper support. Give your body time to reawaken. It can take 3 to 6 months or longer for your natural cycle to reestablish. Everything in this guide is here to serve that process. Address the root of your problems and finally let your body work naturally, the way it was always supposed to, without synthetics interfering with it. I assure you, you will feel better than you ever have.

Module 16 · Cycle Syncing

Beginner Cycle Syncing Guide

Cycle syncing is the practice of aligning your lifestyle with the natural rhythms of your hormonal cycle food, movement, work, social life, and rest.

"Cycle syncing is not a rigid protocol, it is simply the practice of listening to your body and responding with care."
📅 Your Complete Cycle Syncing Reference
AreaMenstrual ❄Follicular 🌿Ovulation ☀Luteal 🍂
EnergyLow, inwardRising, freshPeak, magneticDeclining, earthy
MovementWalking, gentle stretching, yoga, restHiking, dancing, cycling in nature, pilates, swimming, strength trainingHiking, dancing, open water swimming, longer walks, strength training, energetic movement that feels joyfulWalking, gentle yoga, slow cycling, stretching, swimming
Food focusWarm, iron-rich, nourishingLight, fresh, liver foodsZinc, antioxidantsMagnesium, complex carbs
Social lifeSolitude, close peopleSmall social, curiousSocialize freely, leadSelective, then withdraw
Tasks & energyRest, reflect, take things slowPlan, start new things, brainstormTake on challenges, have important conversations, show up fullyComplete tasks, tie up loose ends, focus on details, organize
Self-careRest, heat, warm bathsTry new things, exploreConnect, celebrateNesting, boundaries, quiet

✦ Story Time ✦

Right now, I am on day 18 of my cycle. I ovulated, and I am slowly moving toward my luteal phase, since my cycle personally runs a little longer, usually around 30 to 32 days. Honestly, I did not even know exactly which day I was on, I just noticed my hunger and want for sweeter food increasing. That is when it clicked: I am probably entering my luteal phase. So I checked, and yes, I was right.

Most of my cycle I just have fruit in the morning, light and hydrating, and I feel great! But this time I felt hungrier earlier in the morning, so I ate more. That is what a healthy relationship with your body looks like, simply listening. I also reached for more dark chocolate, more raw honey, sweeter fruit, and more hydration too, and craved more carb-rich foods, so I had some sweet potatoes and potatoes yesterday. I felt thirstier, had some coconut water, and just followed what my body was asking for.

What I have also noticed is that once your body comes back into true balance, you genuinely do not feel hungry all month in a way that demands five meals a day. I am personally not a fan of that approach, it is not natural or ancestral to eat three large meals plus snacks in between. There is a common message out there telling women to "make your body feel safe" by eating constantly throughout the day. And yes, certain phases of your cycle will genuinely bring more hunger. But a lot of the time, that much snacking and eating simply is not what your body actually wants.

So many days I just have fruit in the morning and one big, nourishing meal, and I feel great. When that meal is nutrient dense, my body gets everything it needs. I do not have to snack five times a day, and I do not have to eat three huge meals either. I think this is a real misconception many women are sharing right now, often shaped by what we are taught in nutrition programs or in school, in a time when we have access to more food than any generation before us ever did. Eating five meals a day with snacks in between is not inherently natural, and it will not make you healthier or more fulfilled.

So the simplest thing you can do is listen to your body. Your hormones shift, your needs shift, and your job is just to eat nutrient-dense meals, enough that you feel satisfied and are not left hungry again soon after.
How to Start ~ The Beginner Version
1
Rest More During Your Period

This alone will change your relationship with your cycle. Cancel the gym class. Go to bed earlier. Make warm food. This is not you being lazy, it is actually you being intelligent. You need to stop pushing through exhaustion and calling it discipline.

2
Schedule Demanding Tasks During Follicular and Ovulatory Phases

Important conversations, new projects, challenges, anything that requires your full energy → schedule these when your estrogen and testosterone are high. You will show up better and it will feel more natural.

3
Increase Nourishment in the Luteal Phase

When cravings hit before your period, do not feel bad about yourself. Your body genuinely needs more nourishment in this phase. Eat more protein, magnesium-rich foods, and natural carbohydrates from fruit and root vegetables.

☀️ Simple Ways to Support Circulation During Your Cycle

A simple habit many women find comforting during their cycle (especially during menstruation) is keeping their body warm, particularly their feet and lower abdomen. Warmth can encourage relaxation, support circulation, and help some women feel more comfortable when experiencing cramps or tension.

Try:

  • 🧦 Wearing warm socks
  • 🍵 Drinking warm beverages
  • 🛁 Taking a warm bath (Epsom salts, magnesium flakes, lavender, or ginger)
  • ♨️ Using a heating pad on the lower abdomen
  • 🚶‍♀️ Gentle movement such as walking
  • 🥘 Eating regular, nourishing meals
Module 17 · Action Plan

Your 30-Day Cycle Health Action Plan

Small, consistent actions compound into transformation. This is not about being perfect. It is about showing up for yourself, one day at a time.

Before You Begin

Small, consistent actions compound into transformation. If you miss a day, simply pick up where you left off.

As I mentioned throughout this guide, the basics genuinely do most of the work: morning sunlight, restful sleep, eating real, natural foods, drinking clean filtered water, eating seasonally, and spending time with people you love. Regulating your stress as much as possible matters more than most people realize too, between social media, comparison, and constant information overload, stress is genuinely one of the biggest challenges we face today. Get those foundations right and healthy hormones will follow.

But some people, on the other hand, just like having it written down on paper, a clear plan with exactly what they should do and when. That is exactly why I created the 30-day plan below. Think of it as your starting point, a simple, guided way to begin swapping, resetting, and building momentum one day at a time.

Week 1 ~ Foundation & Awareness
Days 1–7
Day 1
Note the first day of your current cycle phase. Download a tracking app or begin a cycle journal.
Day 2
Swap your cooking oil for ghee or coconut oil. Eliminate seed oils from your kitchen.
Day 3
Drink 2–3 cups of ginger, chamomile, or red raspberry leaf tea today.
Day 4
Add a pinch of real salt to your morning water.
Day 5
Eat a protein-rich breakfast and see how you feel. Personally, I eat fruit until noon most of the time and it works great for me, especially in spring and summer. See how you feel and experiment. Your body will tell you what it needs.
Day 6
Go outside within one hour of waking for 10–15 minutes of morning sunlight. No sunglasses.
Day 7
Check your skincare and deodorant ingredients. Identify one product to swap for a cleaner option this week.
Week 2 ~ Nourishment & Gut
Days 8–14
Day 8
Add one fermented food to your day: raw yogurt, kefir, sauerkraut, or raw cheese. See how your body responds to different raw dairy products. Kefir is often better tolerated than cheese, so start there.
Day 9
Cook a nourishing bone broth or vegetable broth, or buy a quality version. Drink one cup daily this week.
Day 10
Take a 10–15 minute walk after your largest meal of the day. Do this every day for the rest of the plan.
Day 11
Add magnesium-rich foods today → dark chocolate 85%+, or make your own homemade chocolate (seriously the best thing you will ever make). I shared how on my YouTube channel in the video "I Swapped These 9 Things and My Hormones Finally Balanced." Note your mood and sleep quality.
Day 12
Swap plastic containers and water bottles for glass or stainless steel where possible.
Day 13
Eat grass-fed meat or pasture-raised eggs today. Focus on quality animal protein for hormone building blocks.
Day 14
Observe your cervical mucus today. Note what you see. This is your hormonal landscape so start learning how to read it.
Week 3 ~ Liver & Sleep
Days 15–21
Day 15
Start your day with warm lemon water. Supports liver function and bile production. Do this every morning.
Day 16
Add one bitter food before or with your lunch: arugula, radicchio, artichoke, or dandelion greens. Bitter taste is one of the most underrated tastes and plays a crucial role in enzyme production, digestion, gut health, and liver detoxification. Try to include it consistently during this phase of your cycle.
Day 17
Set a consistent bedtime and wake time for this week. Same time every night, even on weekends. When your sleep rhythm stabilizes, your body will naturally begin to get tired earlier and wake earlier. Note your sleep quality and morning mood.
Day 18
Take magnesium glycinate (200–400mg) before bed tonight. Note your sleep quality and morning mood.
Day 19
Eliminate screens for one hour before bed. Replace with reading, journaling, or conversation. Do this for the rest of the plan.
Day 20
Swap your conventional laundry detergent for a natural option. Add white distilled vinegar as fabric softener.
Day 21
Practice deep breathing twice today for 5 minutes, or try a morning meditation session. Even once is enough. Notice how you feel afterwards.
Week 4 ~ Integration & Reflection
Days 22–30
Day 22
Review your cycle journal from the past three weeks. What patterns are you starting to see?
Day 23
Identify your current cycle phase and choose your movement accordingly not based on routine but on your body's signal.
Day 24
Cook a nourishing meal using seasonal, local produce. Connect your food to the earth and the season you are in.
Day 25
Write three things your body did well this month. Your body is constantly working for you even when you do not notice. Acknowledge its intelligence and thank it for that.
Day 26
Share one thing you have learned from this guide with a woman you love. Teaching accelerates your own integration.
Day 27
Identify your one biggest cycle symptom. Write down three root cause possibilities from Module 7. Choose one to focus on next month.
Day 28
Plan next month's nourishment around your predicted cycle phases. What phase will you be in each week?
Day 29
If you have tried all three cycle teas, experiment with a new tea or herbal blend.
Day 30
If you enjoy journaling, write about your cycle this month. In a year you will see so much difference and improvement. Journaling is a powerful practice for any area of life, not just your cycle.
Module 18 · Questions

Frequently Asked Questions

How long will it take to see changes in my cycle? ▾
Most women begin noticing shifts within one full cycle (28–35 days). Significant changes reduced cramping, better mood, more regular cycles typically take 3 months of consistent support. True hormonal rebalancing, especially after years of pill use, can take 6–12 months. Be patient and consistent. Your body is not broken, it is healing.
Can I use this guide if I'm on birth control? ▾
It depends on what part of the guide you are referring to. The foundational pillars, including nutrition, stress support, sleep, blood sugar balance, reducing toxic load, and gut health, will still benefit you and you should absolutely use them. However, the phase-specific recommendations do not apply. The pill suppresses your natural cycle entirely. There is no real ovulation, no natural estrogen or progesterone rhythm, and no real phases to sync with. The four-phase approach, cycle tracking by symptoms, BBT charting, and seed cycling are all irrelevant while on the pill.

My honest recommendation is to consider coming off the pill if you can. Use the foundational modules of this guide to build your health now so that when you are ready, your body has everything it needs to reawaken. Everything here is designed to help you get to a place where your natural cycle is strong, symptom-free, and something you can actually work with.
What happens to my cycle after coming off the pill? ▾
Coming off the pill requires your hypothalamus, pituitary, and ovaries to reestablish communication. Most women see their natural cycle return within 3–6 months. Some take up to a year. Common experiences include: irregular cycles, acne, mood shifts, and changes in flow. This is your natural hormones returning not a permanent state. Support your body with all the practices in this guide and give it time.
My cycle is very irregular. Where do I start? ▾
Start with the three foundations: blood sugar balance (Module 12), stress, nervous system support (Module 10), and nourishment through natural nutrient-dense foods and restorative sleep (Module 8 and 11). Irregular cycles are most commonly driven by chronic stress, under-eating, or blood sugar instability. Begin tracking everything so you can see patterns. If cycles remain absent or very irregular after 3 months of lifestyle support, work with a functional medicine practitioner to investigate further.
Is raw dairy safe? ▾
Raw dairy from healthy, grass-fed cows has been consumed by humans for thousands of years and contains beneficial enzymes, probiotics, and fat-soluble vitamins destroyed by pasteurization. Research the legal status in your country and source from certified farms. If raw dairy is not accessible, high-quality full-fat pasteurized organic dairy is a good alternative.
Do I need to take supplements? ▾
Food first, always. The vast majority of what your cycle needs can be addressed through diet, lifestyle, and reducing your toxic load. That said, these are nutrients most people are deficient in regardless of diet, and they are worth knowing about:

NutrientWhy It Matters
☀ Vitamin DHormone production, immune function, mood regulation
🌿 MagnesiumPMS relief, sleep, nervous system, progesterone support
🏟 Omega-3 fatty acidsReduces inflammation, lowers prostaglandins, supports mood
🩸 IronLost during menstruation, essential for energy and thyroid
🥚 Vitamin B12Energy, nervous system, red blood cell production
🦪 ZincOvulation, follicle development, immune function
🦋 IodineThyroid health, metabolic function, breast tissue health
🌿 Folate (B9)Cell repair, hormone balance, essential pre-pregnancy
🥜 SeleniumThyroid function, antioxidant protection, fertility
🍇 BoronSupports estrogen metabolism, bone health, brain function

Always prioritize getting these from food first. If supplementing, research quality brands and choose third-party tested options. One brand I personally trust and have found to be excellent quality is Global Healing Institute.
Why does my skin break out before my period? ▾
Pre-period breakouts are driven by the estrogen-to-progesterone ratio shift in the late luteal phase, which can trigger increased sebum production. But the root cause goes deeper: excess estrogen from poor liver clearance or gut issues, elevated androgens, and blood sugar instability all worsen this pattern. Address the gut-liver axis, reduce sugar, and support your skin from the inside out with zinc-rich foods, adequate vitamin A from liver and eggs, omega-3 fats, and silica-rich foods like cucumber and leafy greens which support collagen and skin structure. Getting daily sunlight is also deeply underrated for skin health, it regulates sebum production, supports vitamin D synthesis, and calms the inflammatory response that drives breakouts. And manage stress, chronically elevated cortisol directly worsens skin inflammation and breakouts.
Module 19 · Quick Reference

Quick Reference Cheat Sheets

Save or screenshot these. Over time you will not even need a cheat sheet. You will naturally listen to your body and know how to support it best without thinking about it every single day.

❄ Menstrual Phase

  • Rest, slow walks, gentle yoga
  • Iron-rich foods beef, lamb, liver
  • Ginger tea for cramps
  • Warm soups and stews
  • Magnesium for cramping
  • Early bedtime, extra sleep
  • Say no to obligations freely

🌿 Follicular Phase

  • Increase exercise intensity gradually
  • Fresh foods, salads, fermented foods
  • Plan and start new projects
  • Schedule social events
  • Zinc-rich foods for follicle health
  • Liver-supporting foods bitter greens
  • Try something new energy is fresh

☀ Ovulatory Phase

  • Peak performance workouts
  • Important meetings and presentations
  • Antioxidant-rich foods, zinc
  • Connect, socialize, be visible
  • Hydrate well for cervical mucus
  • Enjoy your natural magnetism

🍂 Luteal Phase

  • Moderate exercise, then gentle
  • Magnesium food and supplement
  • Complex carbs (root vegetables)
  • Protect your energy, set limits
  • Prioritize sleep; magnesium before bed
  • Reduce sugar; worsens PMS dramatically
  • Finish and complete tasks detail phase

🍵 Cycle Teas

  • Ginger; cramps (prostaglandin inhibitor)
  • Red raspberry leaf; uterine toner
  • Chamomile; nervous system, sleep
  • Nettle; iron, minerals (menstrual)
  • Peppermint; bloating, digestion
  • Spearmint; androgen support
  • Lemon balm; anxiety, sleep (luteal)

☀ Daily Foundations

  • Prioritize fresh, raw, natural food
  • Morning sunlight within 1 hour of waking
  • Walk after meals
  • Screens off a few hours before bed
  • Watch the sunset before bed
  • Warm lemon water on waking

🌿 Liver Support Foods

  • Bitter greens arugula, dandelion
  • Cruciferous vegetables broccoli, cabbage
  • Lemon water daily
  • Beets bile production
  • Artichokes liver protection
  • Garlic and onions sulfur compounds
  • Adequate protein liver enzyme production

✓ Healthy Period Checklist

  • Cycle 24–35 days long
  • Period lasts 3–7 days
  • Bright red blood, minimal clots
  • Mild discomfort, not debilitating pain
  • Light to moderate flow
  • Mild PMS, not severe mood disruption
  • Ovulation confirmed by BBT or mucus

⚠ Avoid Always

  • Seed oils
  • Ultra-processed foods
  • Refined sugar especially pre-period
  • Plastic products
  • Tap water
  • Conventional beauty, body and hair care products, especially those with synthetic fragrances
  • Conventional plastic period products
  • Under-eating or over-training
  • Excess alcohol. A glass of quality wine or homemade alcohol shared with a meal from time to time can be part of a healthy, balanced lifestyle, especially when enjoyed slowly and in good company. Even in biblical times, wine was commonly shared with meals and often diluted with water.
  • Smoking
Module 20 · Final Words & Brand Recommendations

You Were Always Meant to Feel This Good.

This is the beginning of something real. I genuinely hope this guide helps you feel at home in your body again.

I know I've covered a lot in this course, but don't overcomplicate it. If you eat real, natural food, sleep well, get sunlight, move your body, manage stress, and reduce your exposure to hormone-disrupting chemicals, your body will heal and thrive.

This isn't about doing everything perfectly, obsessing over cycle syncing, or tracking every detail of your life. It's about returning to the foundations.

Focus on the big rocks first, trust your body, and everything else will take care of itself.

These are products I personally use, love, and trust. I only recommend what I genuinely believe in.

Clean skincare and natural deodorant. One of my most trusted brands for hormone-safe body care.

Natural handmade soaps and body care.

My favourite countertop reverse osmosis water filter. No installation needed and removes the vast majority of contaminants from your water.

A water vortexer that restructures and energizes your water after filtering, bringing back the natural movement that still, piped water loses.

Araza Beauty

Clean, non-toxic makeup brand I personally love and trust.

Oura Ring

My personal favourite health tracking device. Tracks your cycle, body temperature, readiness, and sleep in one place. Worth every penny.

Blue Light Glasses

UK Blue Light Glasses. Wear these in the evening to protect melatonin production and support better sleep. Essential if you use screens after sunset.

Global Healing Institute

One of the best supplement brands I have found. Clean, high quality, and effective. I particularly love their Chemical and Heavy Metal Cleanse Program.
